g.data-narration: Welcome to this lesson on synchronizing physical clocks in distributed systems.
In distributed systems, physical clocks maintain temporal ordering and ensure consistency across multiple machines.
g.text: Physical clocks provide absolute time reference based on UTC (Coordinated Universal Time).
Without synchronization, events may appear in wrong order → data inconsistencies.
Critical for: financial transactions, distributed databases, time-sensitive applications.

g.data-narration: Physical clocks use crystal oscillators that run at slightly different frequencies, causing clock drift over time.
g.text: Physical clocks use crystal oscillators that generate periodic signals.
No oscillator is perfect → each crystal oscillates at slightly different frequency.
Drift rate: ~10-100 ppm (parts per million). Two synced clocks can drift seconds per day!

g.data-narration: Three key requirements: clocks must agree on time, progress monotonically, and be accurate to UTC.
Internal synchronization ensures nodes agree with each other; external synchronization ensures agreement with UTC.
Cristian's algorithm uses a centralized time server; client estimates one-way delay as half the round-trip time.
g.text: Centralized approach: Single time server maintains authoritative time.
RTT = T3 - T1 | One-way delay ≈ RTT/2

g.data-narration: The clock offset formula: offset = server_time + (RTT/2) - client_receive_time.
g.text: Clock Offset = Server Time + (RTT ÷ 2) - Client Receive Time

g.data-narration: The Berkeley algorithm uses a coordinator that polls machines and computes consensus average time.
g.text: Decentralized approach: Coordinator daemon actively polls all machines and computes consensus.

g.data-narration: Berkeley computes average of all machine times and sends adjustment values to each machine.
Network Time Protocol (NTP) is the industry standard with hierarchical stratum levels for internet-wide sync.
g.text: Industry standard for Internet clock synchronization. Hierarchical organization into strata.

g.data-narration: NTP uses four timestamps: offset equals half the sum of receive-transmit differences, delay is total minus internal difference.
Clock stepping sets time directly but can violate monotonicity; clock slewing adjusts rate gradually for safety.
g.text: Set clock directly to correct time.

g.data-narration: Key points: Physical clocks drift requiring resync; Cristian uses server with RTT/2 assumption; Berkeley computes consensus.
g.text: 1. Physical clocks drift due to oscillator imperfections → must resync periodically
2. Cristian's: Centralized server, assumes symmetric delay (RTT÷2), simple but single point of failure
3. Berkeley: Computes consensus average, robust against faulty coordinator, no UTC required
